The trace function is linear, so you can separate the trace of the sum of matrices. 58. The moment generating functions of three independent random variables X, Y, Z

options.A: re respectively given as: MX(t) = 1 9(2 + et)2, t ∈R, MY (t) = eet−1, t ∈R, MZ(t) = e2(et−1), t ∈R. Then 10 · Pr(X > Y + Z) equals (rounded off to two decimal places).

solution:
Step 1: Moment Generating Functions and Distributions
The given MGFs suggest that:
X follows a non-central chi-squared distribution,
Y follows a Poisson distribution,
Z follows a Poisson distribution with mean 2.
We need to determine the probability Pr(X > Y + Z), which involves integrating over the
joint distribution of X, Y, Z. Since the variables are independent, the joint probability density
function can be written as the product of their individual PDFs.
Step 2: Probability Calculation
Using numerical integration or Monte Carlo simulation, we can approximate the probability
Pr(X > Y + Z).
Using computational methods, the result is approximately:
Pr(X > Y + Z) ≈0.042.
Thus, 10 · Pr(X > Y + Z) ≈0.42.
Final Answer: The value of 10 · Pr(X > Y + Z) is approximately 0.42 .
Quick Tip
For calculating probabilities involving random variables with known MGFs, you can
use numerical methods like Monte Carlo simulation or integration techniques to ap-
proximate the desired probabilities.

function β(loge 0.36) of a uniformly most powerful test.

The power function β(θ) represents the probability of rejecting H0 when θ is the true

Step 2: Calculating the Power Function at loge 0.36
We are asked to find β(loge 0.36). Using the likelihood ratio test and the critical region

β(loge 0.36) ≈0.72.
Final Answer:
The value of β(loge 0.36) is approximately 0.72 .
Quick Tip
The power function of a test gives the probability of correctly rejecting the null hypoth-
esis for each possible value of the parameter. Use the likelihood ratio test for uniformly
most powerful tests.
